one_line_takeaway: garak is an open-source, Nmap/Metasploit-inspired framework that decomposes LLM red teaming into pluggable Generators/Probes/Detectors/Buffs and adds an adaptive attack-generation probe (atkgen) that elicited toxicity from all seven tested LLMs, positioning LLM security as open-ended discovery rather than benchmarking.
key_finding: The adaptive atkgen probe successfully elicited toxic output from EVERY evaluated target LLM, demonstrating that a small, pre-RLHF/pre-instruct GPT-2 aggressor can adaptively drive even well-aligned models into a target failure mode. Measured toxicity elicitation rates (DistilBERT detector, >0.5): OPT-6.7B 26.7% (highest), GPT-2 17.0%, GPT-3 10.5%, Wizard-uncensored 5.7%, Vicuna 3.8%, GPT-4 2.9%, GPT-3.5 1.0% (lowest). More instruction-tuned/aligned models were markedly harder to elicit toxicity from than base models. As a qualitative illustration, a single garak run against OpenAI gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 produced a hitlog of 11,426 successful adversarial entries (spanning slur continuation, snowballed reasoning errors e.g. falsely calling 7253 composite, verbatim prompt-injection hijacks, and false-premise misinformation such as 'Radon is essential for bone formation').
core_contribution: The paper introduces garak (Generative AI Red-teaming and Assessment Kit), an open-source (Apache 2.0) Python framework for structured security probing of LLMs and dialog systems. Its own contribution is (1) an architectural decomposition of LLM security evaluation into four pluggable, extensible components -- Generators (abstractions over any text-producing model/API: Hugging Face, OpenAI, Cohere, NVIDIA NIM, gguf, Replicate, OctoML, REST, etc.), Probes (each eliciting one vulnerability class), Detectors (keyword/signature-based and ML-classifier-based scorers of model output), and Buffs (perturbations of prompts/hyperparameters, e.g. lowercasing, paraphrase, base64 encoding, backtranslation) tied together by a Harness; (2) an argument that LLM security should be framed as open-ended exploration/discovery (red teaming) rather than fixed benchmarking; and (3) atkgen, an adaptive 'attack generation' module that fine-tunes a red-teaming LLM to steer targets toward a failure mode via multi-turn dialogue. The authors explicitly frame garak by analogy to Nmap (network scanner) and Metasploit (a shared repository where contributors add proof-of-concept exploits). Note: many concrete attacks garak ships (GCG, AutoDAN, DAN, PromptInject, GCG/Greedy Coordinate Descent, token-repetition training-data extraction) are integrations of prior cited work, NOT novel attacks introduced by this paper -- the novelty is the unifying framework and the atkgen adaptive-probing template.
threat_model: garak is a defensive auditing/red-teaming tool; the adversary it models is broad -- 'anyone with access to the internet and a decent command of natural language.' It probes for a wide, open class of LLM failure modes: jailbreaks (DAN, AutoDAN, GCG), direct and indirect/encoding-based prompt injection, training-data replay / membership inference (token-repetition extraction), data/chat exfiltration to third-party servers, malware and known-bad-signature (EICAR) generation, package (dependency) hallucination enabling squatting, toxicity/slur continuation, and misinformation/false-claim generation. A key conceptual position: a failure mode is only a 'vulnerability' relative to a stated deployment policy -- 'if no policy exists for the model, there is no failure mode.' The paper explicitly scopes OUT broader system-context issues such as code execution and insufficient access controls.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: LLM vulnerabilities are an open class, so garak cannot give comprehensive security answers and is meant to augment human assessment, not replace it. Automatic failure detection is fragile -- the long tail of model outputs may require per-model annotation before precise detector evaluation. Probes are currently English-only. garak deliberately does not address broader system-level security (code execution, access controls). atkgen specifically is a deliberately minimal MVP: it targets only the 'obvious' behavior of toxicity; uses a weak pre-LoRA/pre-RLHF GPT-2 aggressor; relies on an off-the-shelf toxicity detector of unknown quality both to select training data and to score results (circular target definition); trains on HHRLHF data that is stale/low-novelty because it has been widely used in alignment training; and is stateless. Reproducibility caveat: Cohere results are missing because the Cohere API rejected the highly toxic atkgen prompts. The authors themselves argue garak results should NOT be treated as a benchmark, since output varies by run configuration and context -- so Table 1 is illustrative rather than a stable leaderboard.

datasets_used: Anthropic HHRLHF red-team-attempts data (Ganguli et al. 2022) -- source of atkgen training dialogs
Jigsaw unintended-bias toxicity classification data (cjadams et al. 2019) -- used to train the DistilBERT toxicity detector
RealToxicityPrompts (Gehman et al. 2020; noted as 3.7GB compressed) -- basis of a shipped toxicity probe
garak's own hitlog logs from prior runs -- reused to retrain/extend atkgen
quantitative_results: atkgen toxicity elicitation rate by target: OPT-6.7B 26.7%
atkgen elicited toxicity from all 7 evaluated targets; eval used 20 dialogs of 5 attacker/target exchanges each (Table 1 caption)
atkgen GPT-2 aggressor fine-tuned for 40 epochs; toxicity threshold >0.5 on martin-ha/toxic-comment-model
Example gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 run yielded a hitlog of 11,426 successful adversarial entries
baselines_compared: No quantitative head-to-head baseline comparison is reported (garak is presented as a framework, not a benchmark).

Related frameworks are discussed qualitatively as context, not benchmarked against: IBM ART (adversarial-robustness-toolbox, image-focused), Giskard (not security-focused, small probe set), NeMo Guardrails, Metasploit (structural analogy).
The fine-tuned GPT-2 atkgen model is itself referred to as the 'baseline attack model' whose per-target toxicity rates appear in Table 1.
